description:
You are acting as an artifact-manager who has to curate and catalogue a small binary repository that is located in the home directory.  
Everything you do must be reproducible in a plain Linux shell without root privileges.

Initial set-up that already exists when you start the task
----------------------------------------------------------
1. Directory structure (all paths are absolute):
   • /home/user/artifacts  
     ├── source/                (contains the input files)  
     ├── releases/              (currently empty)  
     └── staging/               (currently empty)  

2. Exact content of the three files that are already inside /home/user/artifacts/source/  
   • /home/user/artifacts/source/binary1.bin  – exactly 250 bytes, consisting of the ASCII string “BIN1\n” repeated 50 times  
   • /home/user/artifacts/source/binary2.bin  – exactly 250 bytes, consisting of the ASCII string “BIN2\n” repeated 50 times  
   • /home/user/artifacts/source/readme.txt   – exactly 48 bytes, containing the single line:  
     These are sample binaries for compression test.\n

Goal
----
A. Create two compressed archives from the *source* directory:  
   1. binaries_backup.tar.gz – must hold ONLY binary1.bin and binary2.bin.  
      • It has to be created in such a way that, once un-compressed, the two files appear in exactly the same relative paths they had in <source>.  
   2. docs.zip – must hold ONLY readme.txt, again preserving its relative path.

B. Move both archives into the directory /home/user/artifacts/releases/.

C. Extract binaries_backup.tar.gz into /home/user/artifacts/staging/ so that, after extraction, the staging directory contains binary1.bin and binary2.bin (and no other files or sub-directories).

D. Produce a catalogue file called /home/user/artifacts/release_log.txt that lists a one-line summary for every archive stored in /home/user/artifacts/releases/.

Required format of /home/user/artifacts/release_log.txt
-------------------------------------------------------
The file must contain exactly two lines, one for each archive, **in the order shown below**:

binaries_backup.tar.gz | SIZE_BYTES | SHA256_DIGEST  
docs.zip | SIZE_BYTES | SHA256_DIGEST  

Formatting rules:
1. Use the literal archive file names shown above (no paths).
2. The three fields on each line are separated by a single space, a vertical bar, and another single space:  " | ".
3. SIZE_BYTES is the exact size in bytes of the archive **as it exists inside /home/user/artifacts/releases/**, written as a base-10 integer with no thousands separators.
4. SHA256_DIGEST is the lower-case, 64-character hexadecimal SHA-256 checksum of the archive **as it exists inside /home/user/artifacts/releases/**.
5. Each line must be terminated by a single newline (LF) character.  No extra blank lines at the end of the file.

Verification expectation
------------------------
1. /home/user/artifacts/releases/binaries_backup.tar.gz   → file exists and is a valid gzip-compressed tar archive that contains only binary1.bin and binary2.bin.  
2. /home/user/artifacts/releases/docs.zip                 → file exists and contains only readme.txt.  
3. /home/user/artifacts/staging/                          → contains *exactly* binary1.bin and binary2.bin, byte-for-byte identical to the originals under /home/user/artifacts/source/.  
4. /home/user/artifacts/release_log.txt                   → exists and follows the format rules above; the SIZE_BYTES and SHA256_DIGEST fields must match the *actual* properties of the archives found in /home/user/artifacts/releases/.

Hints (do NOT show exact commands)
----------------------------------
• You will probably need the tar, gzip, zip, sha256sum, and stat utilities that are available on a standard Linux system.  
• Remember to preserve relative paths inside the archives so that extraction recreates the files properly.  
• Make sure you write (or overwrite) release_log.txt after the archives are moved into the releases directory; otherwise the sizes and hashes will be wrong.  
• The SHA-256 digest should be calculated on the archive file itself, *not* on the individual files inside the archive.  
• Be careful about the exact “space-bar-pipe-space” separators and the line order in release_log.txt – the automated test is strict about that.
truth:
Initial filesystem state that exists *before* the agent begins:

Directories:
  /home/user/artifacts/
  /home/user/artifacts/source/
  /home/user/artifacts/releases/
  /home/user/artifacts/staging/

Files and byte-accurate contents:
  /home/user/artifacts/source/binary1.bin
     (ASCII string "BIN1\n" repeated 50 times → 250 bytes total)
  /home/user/artifacts/source/binary2.bin
     (ASCII string "BIN2\n" repeated 50 times → 250 bytes total)
  /home/user/artifacts/source/readme.txt
     (ASCII line "These are sample binaries for compression test.\n" → 48 bytes)

Nothing else exists under /home/user/artifacts/.

Expected artefacts AFTER correct completion:

1. /home/user/artifacts/releases/binaries_backup.tar.gz  
   • gzip-compressed tar archive  
   • When listed with tar -tzf, it shows exactly:  
       ./binary1.bin  
       ./binary2.bin  
   • The content bytes of both files inside the archive match exactly the originals.

2. /home/user/artifacts/releases/docs.zip  
   • Zip archive whose zipinfo output lists exactly:  
       binary1.bin  ← (should NOT be present)  
       binary2.bin  ← (should NOT be present)  
       readme.txt   ← (only this)  

3. /home/user/artifacts/staging/  
   • Contains only binary1.bin and binary2.bin, each byte-for-byte identical to their counterparts in /home/user/artifacts/source/.

4. /home/user/artifacts/release_log.txt  
   • Two lines, precisely:  
       binaries_backup.tar.gz | <SIZE1> | <SHA1>  
       docs.zip | <SIZE2> | <SHA2>  
   • <SIZE1>, <SIZE2> are the decimal sizes of the files named in #1 and #2.  
   • <SHA1>, <SHA2> are the lowercase SHA-256 digests of those two files.

The automated test will:
  a. Re-compute SHA-256 checksums of the two archives in /home/user/artifacts/releases/  
  b. Parse release_log.txt, extract the numeric SIZE_BYTES and SHA256_DIGEST fields, and ensure they exactly match the actual file metadata and checksums.  
  c. Verify that staging contains only the two binary files whose SHA-256 digests match those in source.  
  d. Confirm that no extra files were created outside the expected directories.
